runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: Recall: typical labor-supply elasticity parameterization and low-income subsistence constraints imply smaller substitution responses in bottom deciles.
Recall: microsimulation models often use heterogeneous behavioral elasticities with reduced responsiveness for very low earnings.
Recall: bounds and effective-marginal-wage interpretation in tax-benefit behavioral modules generally yield elasticities well below 1 for decile 1.
runs.reasoningSummary: I currently endorse a modest substitution-response elasticity in earnings decile 1 because very low earners face tighter budget constraints, benefit program complexities, and likely limited margin for substitution; however, some responsiveness remains, so the median is around 0.25 with an upper tail below ~1.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model (elasticity measuring how primary-earner labor supply responds to bounded changes in the effective marginal wage in decile 1).
citations: Standard labor-supply elasticity ranges used in microsimulations (work by Saez, Eissa-Gong, Chetty/Gruber-Saez on labor supply responses)
Empirical findings that substitution elasticities for low-income/primary earners are typically modest (wage elasticity often relatively small but nonzero)
Calibration practice: decile-specific elasticities often constrained by observed extensive/marginal responses
runs.reasoningSummary: I expect decile-1 primary earners to have a moderate substitution-response to changes in effective marginal wages, with substantial mass on small-to-intermediate elasticities and an upper tail reflecting less constrained responsiveness; hence a median around 0.35 with plausible 5th–95th range about 0.10–0.80.
